---
title: "AB Testing is an Optimization Problem"
subtitle: "Why Traditional Stopping Rules Are Arbitrary"
author: "Russ Zaliznyak"
date: "2026-05-22"
description: >
  Frequentist stopping rules — run until p < 0.05 — are not derived from any business
  objective. Reframes AB testing as a decision optimization problem and shows how
  Bayesian and sequential methods align stopping criteria with what actually matters.
categories: [AB Testing, Bayesian, Decision Theory, Optimization]
execute:
  echo: false
---

::: {.post-meta}
[In Progress]{.badge-wip}
:::

# Introduction

In 1925, Ronald Fisher chose p < 0.05 as a convenient threshold for analyzing crop yields.
That rule is now the global default for product experimentation — not because it's optimal,
but because it was inherited.

Thirty-one years later, Fisher wrote:

> *"The calculation is absurdly academic, for in fact no scientific worker has a fixed level of significance at which from year to year, and in all circumstances, he rejects hypotheses."*
>
> — *Statistical Methods and Scientific Inference* (1956)

In other words, stopping rules are completely arbitrary without context.
